retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Materials
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramics, particularly MTA, can also be a core filling material. In addition to the aforementioned chemical properties of MTA, it is commonly used as an endodontic material due to its many beneficial characteristics. These include a long set time, a low relative compressive strength, high fracture resistance, and antibacterial and antifungal properties. [26] [27] Furthermore, MTA materials have been consistently proven in the literature to be biocompatible due to their high pH and ability to form hydroxyapatite, an organic substance that promotes bony growth. [28] There are several notable uses of MTA in endodontics, including in the repair of perforations during endodontic treatment, internal root resorption, apexogenesis, and pulp capping procedures. [11] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Core Obturation Materials
retrieved_docs.content: The master cone is inserted into the canal to the working length, followed by the spreader, which, if properly selected, should reach 2 mm shorter. The spreader is then rotated and withdrawn from the canal, and the matching gutta-percha cone is inserted into the vacant space. [39] The process is repeated until the operator cannot insert the spreader deeper than the coronal third of the prepared canal. The excess gutta-percha is seared off with a heated instrument and compacted using a root canal plugger. [5] [41] [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A periapical radiolucency often persists when the root canal treatment cannot control the infection because some clinical steps were not adequately followed, such as insufficient aseptic control, poor instrumentation, inadequate access cavity design, unreached canals, and restoration leakage. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Several key characteristics should be considered when selecting sealers and core obturation materials. Firstly, most sealers are soluble and subject to dissolution when they contact tissue fluids. [32] Additionally, most sealers and core materials irritate the periapical tissues if extruded past the apex, except bioceramic materials, as these materials are biocompatible and rarely act as irritants. [20] [33] Next, sealers differ in their ability to adhere to prepared dentinal walls, with sealers such as ZOE being nonadherent, whereas resin-based sealers are. [9] [34] Lastly, most sealers experience shrinkage upon setting and can create void space between the core material and the dentinal wall, except bioceramic sealers exhibit expansion upon setting. [9] [22] Due to bioceramic materials' enhanced biocompatible and physical properties, they have been indicated and utilized in most cases in modern endodontics. [5] [21] However, using a specific sealer or core material depends entirely on the operator's preference and can be case-specific.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Follow-up appointments are essential to assess the progression and efficacy of treatment. Further management is indicated when a periapical radiolucency remains unchanged after one year of root canal treatment, when it has increased in size, or if it appears in an endodontic-treated tooth without a prior apical disease. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The UltraAC is indicated for anterior teeth with intact crowns and minimal structural loss, particularly when preoperative imaging confirms a single straight canal. These cases often benefit from the use of high magnification or guided access methods to maintain precision while preserving as much tooth structure as possible.

retrieved_docs.content: Extracorporeal lithotripsy; urinary is a non‑invasive therapeutic technique used to address the pathology of حَصاة (urinary stones). By delivering focused shock waves to the stone, the procedure fragments the حَصاة into smaller pieces that can be passed spontaneously, thereby reducing the need for more invasive surgical interventions and improving patient outcomes in the management of urolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1014
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion is classified as dilatation and curettage (D&C) aspiration after delivery or abortion. The procedural category dilatation and curettage (D&C) aspiration after delivery or abortion likewise defines itself as dilatation and curettage (d&c) aspiration after delivery or abortion, indicating that both terms refer to the same obstetric surgical technique used to remove retained products of conception after childbirth or induced abortion. This unified classification supports consistent clinical documentation, coding, and selection of appropriate management strategies.
